coredata.dc:title: A standard procedure for rapid toxicity evaluation of carbon dots both in vitro and in vivo
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Hao
Yang, Yuxiang
Li, Yan
Li, Zijian
Tedesco, Antonio Claudio
Bi, Hong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging class of fluorescent quantum dot nanomaterials that have attracted considerable scientific attention for biomedical or bioimaging applications due to their physicochemical and biochemical properties. With the emergence of massive novel synthetic CDs applying to biomedical fields of science, evaluating their biosafety before any biological application is essential. However, there is no universal protocol or routine procedures for toxicity detection and biosafety assessment of CDs in general biological environments. Herein, we provide an ideal and fast operating system to detect the biotoxicity of CDs, which has been preliminary practiced. Briefly, the obtained CDs will be evaluated by in vitro cytotoxicity assay using cell counting kit-8, lactate dehydrogenase assay kit, and flow cytometry. Meanwhile, the model creature zebrafish is employed to perform in vivo evaluation by measuring body length, hatching rate, heart rate, and morphological observation. Our operating procedure condenses previous scattered biosafety detection methods into a rapid standard evaluation protocol that can be applied to early biotoxicity screening of CDs. This protocol will accelerate CDs biological exploitation and guide future industrialized biosafety assessment in large-scale applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Toxicity
Standard procedure
Rapid evaluation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell and zebrafish embryo preparation (Day 1: cost 2 h + overnight)
CDs treatment and imaging of zebrafish embryos (Day 2: cost 8 h + overnight)
Biotoxicity analysis of zebrafish (Day 5: cost 7 h)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52172033 and 22005280 ). We acknowledge the support from the support of the Key Laboratory of Structure and Functional Regulation of Hybrid Materials of the Ministry of Education , Anhui University , Hefei, Anhui 230601, China. The authors also acknowledge the support from the Key Laboratory of Environment-Friendly Polymer Materials of Anhui Province , Hefei, China, and Key Laboratory of Functional Inorganic Material Chemistry of Anhui Province , Anhui University , Hefei 230601, P. R. China. Animal welfare and experimental procedures were strictly examined and approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of Anhui University.

This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52172033 and 22005280). We acknowledge the support from the support of the Key Laboratory of Structure and Functional Regulation of Hybrid Materials of the Ministry of Education, Anhui University, Hefei, Anhui 230601, China. The authors also acknowledge the support from the Key Laboratory of Environment-Friendly Polymer Materials of Anhui Province, Hefei, China, and Key Laboratory of Functional Inorganic Material Chemistry of Anhui Province, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, P. R. China. Animal welfare and experimental procedures were strictly examined and approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of Anhui University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flow diagram showing the overview of the protocol. Overview of the timing for cytotoxicity detection and biosafety assessment in zebrafish.

Time-course illustration of key steps. A Example of the 96-well plate in CCK-8 assay. b-c Multi-angle photography for the arrangement of two males and three females separated by a divider. d Prepared 1✕E3 buffer. e-f The placement of embryos in the 6-well plate and the image of newborn embryos.

Typical images of cytotoxicity detection. A Cell viability detection performed by CCK-8. b Apoptosis analysis performed by Annexin/PI detection with flow cytometry. Reprinted with permission from ref. 21. Copyright 2023 Elsevier.

Typical images of biosafety assessment in zebrafish. A Survival rate of zebrafish larvae. b Body length of zebrafish larvae. c Heartbeat rate of zebrafish larvae. d Imaging of embryos and zebrafish larvae treated with CDs. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001. Statistical analysis was performed using the t-test. Reprinted with permission from ref. 20. Copyright 2022, American Chemical Society.

head.ce:title: A standard procedure for rapid toxicity evaluation of carbon dots both in vitro and in vivo

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Center of Nanotechnology and Tissue Engineering, Photobiology and Photomedicine Research Group, Faculty of Philosophy, Sciences, and Letters of Ribeirao Preto, University of Sao Paulo, 14040-901, Ribeirao Preto, SP, Brazil

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging class of fluorescent quantum dot nanomaterials that have attracted considerable scientific attention for biomedical or bioimaging applications due to their physicochemical and biochemical properties. With the emergence of massive novel synthetic CDs applying to biomedical fields of science, evaluating their biosafety before any biological application is essential. However, there is no universal protocol or routine procedures for toxicity detection and biosafety assessment of CDs in general biological environments. Herein, we provide an ideal and fast operating system to detect the biotoxicity of CDs, which has been preliminary practiced. Briefly, the obtained CDs will be evaluated by in vitro cytotoxicity assay using cell counting kit-8, lactate dehydrogenase assay kit, and flow cytometry. Meanwhile, the model creature zebrafish is employed to perform in vivo evaluation by measuring body length, hatching rate, heart rate, and morphological observation. Our operating procedure condenses previous scattered biosafety detection methods into a rapid standard evaluation protocol that can be applied to early biotoxicity screening of CDs. This protocol will accelerate CDs biological exploitation and guide future industrialized biosafety assessment in large-scale applications.

ce:section.ce:para:
As an emerging kind of fluorescent quantum dot nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) possess vibrant morphological and physicochemical properties which are composed of graphitized sp
2 carbon core and surface functional groups/organic shells with a size of less than 10 nm [1]. CDs have drawn considerable scientific attention in imaging, diagnostics, and therapy for their distinctive optical properties, low cost, high stability, small size, and appreciable biocompatibility [1,2]. Moreover, the variability and richness of surface functional groups confer CDs as attractive agents in biomedical applications [2,3]. Profit from these advantages, CDs have been widely studied in the biological field over the past decade after the discovery of fluorescent CDs in 2004 [4]. Along with diverse CDs being synthesized, methods for preparing high-quality CDs are summarized, which provides valuable references to standardized preparation in large-scale applications [5]. However, the primary issue facing biological applications is biosafety assessment after the synthesis and purification. Different testing methods of biotoxicity in the literature have their own procedures mixed with the synthetic and purified procedures, employing different biological models in vitro and in vivo, making such confusing and difficult to reproduce or standardize. Thus, a standard protocol is urgently needed for the virtuous development of this emerging area. On the other hand, in the context of numerous new CDs being synthesized worldwide and reported in exponentially growing publications continuously, the lack of rapid and efficient protocol for CDs biotoxicity detection in vitro and in vivo hinders the industrialization development of CDs. Although the guideline of standard toxicity detection for small molecule drugs can be found in manual instruction, it is still indeterminate whether CDs could follow the guideline directly. CDs face a situation with no handbook described to obey as a new nanomedicine.

In the past decades, the main approaches in CDs biosafety evaluation can be categorized into two approaches during the explorations [6]. The cell viability experiments are the conventional measurements that contain a series of standard assays, such as methyl tetrazolium (MTT) and cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) [7,8]. Cells were cultured with the gradient concertation CDs, and corresponding vehicle control was set to compare the biotoxicity. Another approach is based on drug delivery in model creatures, including rodents and zebrafish [9–11]. For instance, the CDs are injected through the mouse tail vein for a certain period. Blood biochemical, hematological, and pathological section analysis towards the heart, liver, kidney, spleen, and lung are executed. In recent years, we have synthesized a series of bio-functional CDs and explored their applications in biology [12–15]. Their minor toxicity is the primary requirement for our subsequent reliable functionalized explorations [16]. With the development of the studies, in vivo investigation of CDs was performed as well [17]. Nevertheless, in the practice of biosafety assessment, the long experimental period of the mouse detection model could not follow the production speed of CDs, leading to jams. We thus introduced the zebrafish model to break this bottleneck. Zebrafish have high genetic homology to humans, rapid reproduction and development, small size, and good transparency during the early growth stage, which is suitable for rapid biotoxicity screening [18]. The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has approved zebrafish as a toxicological detection model for new drug screening [19]. Based on these advantages, we incubated the CDs with newborn embryos and zebrafish larvae. Then, we analyzed morphological images and measured the body length, hatching rate, and heart rate of zebrafish [20–22]. The whole operating procedure costs less time than before and offers a practical solution approach to rapid evaluation. Here, we collected recent universal evaluation methods from our group and other peer researchers and condense them as a rapid biotoxicity detection protocol.

Cytotoxicity detection regents: Cell lines are purchased from the Cell Culture Center of Peking Union Medical College. Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM; Gibico, cat. no. 11965092), stored between 2 °C and 8 °C. Fetal bovine serum (FBS; Gibico, cat. no. 10099141C), added to media as 10 % vol/vol, stored at −80 °C. Sterile penicillin–streptomycin solution, with 10000 units penicillin and 10 mg streptomycin/mL (Gibico, cat. no. 15140122), added to media as 1 % vol/vol, stored at −20 °C. Trypsin (Gibico, cat. no. 15050065), Stored at −20 °C. Trypsin-EDTA (Gibico, cat. no. 25200056), Stored at −20 °C. Dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO; Sigma-Aldrich, cat. no. 276855), Phosphate buffered saline (PBS; Gibico, cat. no. 10010023), Stored between 2 °C and 8 °C. Cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8; Sigma-Aldrich, cat. no. 96992), Stored between 2 °C and 8 °C. Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) assay kit (Sigma-Aldrich, cat. no. MAK066), Stored at −20 °C. Apoptosis detection kit (Sigma-Aldrich, cat. no. APOAF), Stored between 2 °C and 8 °C.

Zebrafish biotoxicity detection reagents: All AB strains of zebrafish (Danio rerio) used in the protocol were wild-type animals. NaCl (Sigma-Aldrich, cat. no. S9888). KCl (Sigma-Aldrich, cat. no. P9541). CaCl2 (Sigma-Aldrich, cat. no. C4901). NaHCO3 (Sigma-Aldrich, cat. no. S6014). Na2HPO4 (Sigma-Aldrich, cat. no. S9763). KH2PO4 (Sigma-Aldrich, cat. no. P0662). MgSO4 (Sigma-Aldrich, cat. no. 208094). MS-222 (Sigma-Aldrich, cat. no. E10521). Low-melting agarose (Sigma-Aldrich, cat. no. A9045). Methylene blue (Sigma-Aldrich, cat. no. 284-M).

All reagents and solvents used in cell experiments must be employed in a clean environment away from bacteria, dust, fumes, gases, and vapors. The procedures for daily animal care and experiments were conducted under the National Research Council's Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals. Animal welfare and experimental procedures were approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of Anhui University.

E3 buffer: Prepare a 50✕ stock in advance. Add 40.07 g NaCl, 2.01 g KCl, 0.18 g Na2HPO4, 0.3 g KH2PO4, 1.67 g CaCl2, 0.6 g MgSO4, 1.76 g NaHCO3, and 0.05 g methylene blue in 1 L of deionized water and mix well in a flask. It can be stored at room temperature (∼25 °C) for months. Dilute to 1✕ in deionized water just before use.
MS-222 anesthetic: Add 0.04 g MS-222 into 100 mL above formulated 1✕ E3 buffer and mix well in a flask. Achieved 0.04 % (wt/wt) MS-222 anesthetic can be stored between 2 °C and 8 °C for months.

According to the manufacturer's instructions, replace the fresh medium and add 10 μL CCK-8 solution into each well of the 96-well plate. After 1 h incubation in the 37 °C incubator, detect the absorbance of the 96-well plate at 450 nm via a microplate reader. Calculate the results of absorbance data as below:
(S1) Cell viability (%) = (ODCDs – ODblank) / (ODcontrol – ODblank) ✕ 100%

ODCDs represent the absorbance of the well-containing medium, CCK-8, and CDs; ODcontrol represents the absorbance of the well-containing medium and CCK-8; ODblank represents the absorbance of the well-only-containing medium. Please set at least 4 parallel samples in each group. Bubbling in the well will affect the accuracy of the absorbance.

According to the manufacturer's instructions, collect the medium from the 24-well plate. Centrifuge at 10000✕ g for 15 min at 4 °C to remove insoluble components. Then, add 50 μL medium into coated wells and mix well with the reaction solution. Measure the initial absorbance of samples in 450 nm via a microplate reader as Ainitial. Incubate the plate at 37 °C and measure the absorbance after 10 min as Afinal. Calculate the results of absorbance data as below:
(S2) LDH activity (U L−1) = (Afinal – Ainitial) / (Ac-final – Ac-initial) ✕ Cstandard

Ac-initial represents the initial absorbance of the well added with CDs; Ac-initial represents the final absorbance of the well added with CDs; Cstandard represents the LDH activity of standard substance.

Collect the medium supernatant from the 6-well plate and gently wash the cells with PBS 2 times. Then, incubate with trypsin for 3 min. Add enough DMEM medium to stop digestion and transfer medium supernatant and digested cells into a sterile centrifuge tube. Centrifuge at 500✕ g for 5 min at 4 °C to remove the supernatant. Wash the cells with PBS and centrifuge at 400✕ g for 5 min at 4 °C again. Abandon the supernatant and add 100 μL to resuspend the cells. According to the manufacturer's instructions, add Annexin-FITC into the cells to incubate for 60 min and then add PI to the cells for 10 min. Use a flow cytometer to detect the cell fluorescence intensity in FITC and APC paths. As dead cells will float, medium supernatant should be also collected and centrifuged.

The standardized procedure can be divided into cytotoxicity detection in vitro and biosafety assessment in vivo. The two-part operation is mutually independent and can be executed simultaneously to accelerate the schedule. In every assay, a negative control containing the vehicle alone should be included in cell experiments. For zebrafish experiments, a negative control that contains the vehicle alone should also be included on each plate. The vehicle acts as a solvent and does not react chemically with other components in the system, nor does it produce color and fluorescence changes in the system. An overview of the protocol is shown in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell and zebrafish embryo preparation (Day 1: cost 2 h + overnight)
ce:section.ce:para: In the cell experimental part, corresponding cell lines are selected for different biological application prospects to conduct cytotoxicity assays. Select cells that are in the logarithmic growth phase. Remove the medium supernatant from the culture dish and gently wash the cells with PBS 2 times. Then, incubate with trypsin-EDTA for 3 min. Add enough DMEM medium to stop digestion and control cell density between 5✕104 and 105 per mL via cell counting. The cells were evenly spread into 96-well plates, 24-well plates, and 12-well plates for subsequent CCK-8 assay, LDH, and flow cytometry, respectively. The outer ring of the 96-well plate should be discarded, and PBS is added to these wells to prevent evaporation (Fig. 2 a). Then, shift the plates into the cell incubator. The cells are cultured in the incubator at 37 °C overnight to ensure they are fully attached.

In the zebrafish experimental part, to collect embryos, arrange male and female zebrafish in a 2 L breeding tank of two males and three females separated by a mesh screen (Fig. 2b and c). The zebrafish are fed during the 14/10 h light/dark cycle in the incubator at 28 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs treatment and imaging of zebrafish embryos (Day 2: cost 8 h + overnight)
ce:section.ce:para: In the cell experimental part, dissolve gradient concentrations of CDs into a medium, such as 0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, and 300 μg mL−1. Water-insoluble CDs can be pre-dissolved in suitable organic reagents as mother liquor and then diluted into the medium. Replace the medium of the plates with the above-prepared medium containing CDs. Then, the cells are kept in the incubator at 37 °C for 24 h.

In the zebrafish experimental part, place the mating groups in fresh water (volume should be about 2 cm of water depth above the bottom of the mating cage). Then, remove the divider to allow the fish to spawn. After 2 h, using a plastic transfer pipette, carefully collected newborn embryos. Subsequently, divide the embryos into two parts and use one part to incubate them into larvae for the following investigations of survival rate, body length, and heartbeat rate. Keep these embryos in the incubator at 28 °C in the E3 buffer (Fig. 2d) for 72 h. Replace 1/2 capacity of the embryo incubation solution every 24 h. The other part is to place about 30 embryos into each well of a 6-well plate bottom for embryo imaging and hatching rate investigation (Fig. 2e and f). Dissolve gradient concentrations of CDs into the E3 buffer, such as 0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, and 300 μg mL−1. Then, add 2 mL above configured E3 buffer into each well of a 6-well plate. Keep the embryos in the incubator at 28 °C. After 4 h incubation, remove part of the embryos into a new plate and carefully use PBS to wash them 2 times. Then, move the plate under the fluorescence microscope and observe the imaging of CDs in embryos with a 4✕ ocular lens.

The cell plates are removed from the incubator for the subsequent cytotoxicity detection. Unbroken mitochondrial function and cell membrane integrity are the main features of living cells [23,24]. Thus, CCK-8 and LDH assays are adapted to detect cell viability, respectively. Except for the above quantitative analysis, intuitive graphical data is also introduced. Flow cytometry is an effective high-throughput cell biological assay. In the Annexin V/PI assay, the dead cell would be stained with Annexin V and PI, and the cell in the process of apoptosis would be stained with Annexin V but not PI. These assays generally test cytotoxicity from an intuitive and quantitative point of view and can be rapidly accomplished.

Our previous studies provide typical images of this application. As shown in Fig. 3 a, manganese-doped graphene quantum dots (Mn-FGQDs) and their control have lower biotoxicity under dark between 0 and 300 μg mL−1. However, light irradiation will stimulate the phototoxicity of Mn-FGQDs, which exhibits good potential in photodynamic therapy (PDT) [21]. Flow cytometry also indicates 300 μg mL−1 Mn-FGQDs have a higher apoptosis rate under light irradiation (Fig. 3b) [21]. These typical images suggest that the application of our protocol is not restricted to biotoxicity evaluation, it may also be used in various cytotoxicity detections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biotoxicity analysis of zebrafish (Day 5: cost 7 h)
ce:section.ce:para: After 72 h incubation, to obtain the hatching rate, count the hatched zebrafish larvae and unhatched embryos treated with gradient concentrations of CDs. Further, the hatched zebrafish larvae without CDs treatment are removed from the incubator for the next biotoxicity assessments. Dissolve gradient concentrations of CDs into the E3 buffer, such as 0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, and 300 μg mL−1. Remove larvae into the above prepared E3 solution and keep the zebrafish larvae in the incubator at 28 °C. Each group should keep at least 30 larvae. After 3 h, remove the larvae to a new plate and carefully use PBS to wash them 2 times with a plastic transfer pipette. Then, anesthetize the zebrafish larvae with prepared MS-222 anesthetic and transfer them to 1 % low-melting agarose for observation. Move the plate under the fluorescence microscope and observe the imaging of CDs in the larvae with a 4✕ ocular lens. Count the survival rate and heartbeat within 60 s of larvae. Use the ruler of the microscope to measure the body length under a 4✕ ocular lens.

Our previous studies also followed this procedure and achieved a credible biosafety assessment of CDs. As shown in Fig. 4 a–c, after incubation for several days, manganese-bonded hyaluronic acid-derived CDs (Mn/HA-CDs) have no biotoxicity on the survival, body length, and heartbeat of zebrafish larvae under 200 μg mL−1 while having a lower effect on survival above 200 μg mL−1 [20]. In addition, the embryos and larvae treated with Mn/HA-CDs are also imaged. As shown in Fig. 4d, Mn/HA-CDs enter through the chorion in embryos and accumulate in the digestive system of the zebrafish larvae, which have little effect on morphology and have better biocompatibility than their controls [20]. Overall, we provide a rapid and effective in vivo method that is appropriate for high throughput screens.

Various precursors and synthetic procedures generate CDs with different sizes, potential, polarity, solubility, and surface groups [25]. The CDs have been widely applied in biomedicine, food chemistry, and agricultural science [26–28]. This protocol can be appropriate for all kinds of CDs in different contexts, especially water-soluble CDs. For CDs that are insoluble in water, it is recommended to dissolve them in a suitable solvent in advance and then dilute them in water. For instance, CDs can be used as photosensitizers, critical elements in the process of PDT. PDT is a kind of phototherapy used in clinical cancer treatment due to its lower toxicity, high selectivity, and non-drug resistance [29]. Compared with the high cytotoxicity under light irradiation, confirming the nontoxicity of CDs without light irradiation is required. By using related methods from this protocol, in the previous studies, we successfully demonstrated the biosafety of our CDs in the dark and detected the photo-induced toxicity during the PDT [20,21]. Except for PDT, our protocol can also be suitable for other bio-applications, including bioimaging, sensing, drug delivery, antibacterial agents, and anticancer agents.

The biosafety evaluation of CDs detailed in this protocol has several advantages, and the procedure can be easily operated in any standard biology laboratory. First, the materials of this method are commonly used, and all the reagents are commercially accessible. Second, every part of the protocol is mature and long-tested, which has been used in the biotoxicity detection of many other materials, small molecules, etc [19,30,31]. Third, the husbandry of zebrafish does not need too many hardware conditions, which helps researchers to increase enough parallel samples simultaneously to obtain convincing results. Fourth, the growth speed of zebrafish is much faster than rodents, leading shorter in vivo experimental period, which is suitable for high throughput screens in biosafety evaluations. Collectively, the described protocol is particularly useful for researchers who need rapid biosafety evaluation of mass CDs. This protocol aims to provide a standard operating procedure to researchers for guiding related biological studies and industrialization development. We hope it will help to standardize the measurement index during the development of CD biosafety evaluation in recent years.

Hao Cai: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Yuxiang Yang: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yan Li: Methodology. Zijian Li: Validation, Funding acquisition. Antonio Claudio Tedesco: Writing – review & editing. Hong Bi: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52172033 and 22005280). We acknowledge the support from the support of the Key Laboratory of Structure and Functional Regulation of Hybrid Materials of the Ministry of Education, Anhui University, Hefei, Anhui 230601, China. The authors also acknowledge the support from the Key Laboratory of Environment-Friendly Polymer Materials of Anhui Province, Hefei, China, and Key Laboratory of Functional Inorganic Material Chemistry of Anhui Province, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, P. R. China. Animal welfare and experimental procedures were strictly examined and approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of Anhui University.

B. Yao, H. Huang, Y. Liu, Z. Kang, Carbon Dots: A Small Conundrum, Trends Chem., 1 (2019) 235-246 https://doi.org/10.1016/j.trechm.2019.02.003.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots in bioimaging, biosensing and therapeutics: a comprehensive review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Wang, H. Cai, G.I.N. Waterhouse, X. Qu, B. Yang, S. Lu, Carbon Dots in Bioimaging, Biosensing and Therapeutics: A Comprehensive Review, Small Sci., 2 (2022) 2200012 https://doi.org/10.1002/smsc.202200012.

Yun, Planted Graphene Quantum Dots for Targeted, Enhanced Tumor Imaging and Long-Term Visualization of Local Pharmacokinetics, Adv. Mater., 35 (2023) https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.202210809.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A graphene quantum dot photodynamic therapy agent with high singlet oxygen generation

Han, A graphene quantum dot photodynamic therapy agent with high singlet oxygen generation, Nat. Commun., 5 (2014) 4596 https://doi.org/10.1038/ncomms5596.

Interfaces, 13 (2021) 54677-54689 https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.1c14674.

Lin, Quaternized carbon quantum dots with broad-spectrum antibacterial activity for the treatment of wounds infected with mixed bacteria, Acta Biomater., 138 (2022) 528-544 https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actbio.2021.11.010.
